---
title: "Programming Fundamentals and Data I/O"
subtitle: "Learn R with Dr. Hu and His Friends"
author: "Yue Hu (Department of Political Science, Tsinghua University)"

webr:
  packages:
    - drhur
    - lubridate
---

{{< include _extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

# Programming Fundamentals and Data I/O {#sec-play-en}

---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter Highlights"}

1. Foundational concepts
2. Basic syntax
3. Data types
4. Data attributes
5. Data output

*Research question*: How does R help researchers store, import, and export data?

:::

## Object-Oriented Programming {#sec-sub_oop-en}

- Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): organizes and encapsulates data through classes and objects
- C++, Java, Python, and R all adopt OOP
- Better than procedure-oriented programming (POP) at handling complex systems with varied needs

---

- Two basic concepts: class (`class`) and object (`object`)
- Class: a general specification for a group of objects (e.g., "horse", "staple food")
- Object: a concrete instance of a class (e.g., "white horse", "potato")
- Property: a feature of the object
- Method: an action that can be applied to objects of this class

---

- Benefit of OOP: classify first, then operate in batch
- Example: dirty shirts and pants → "dirty laundry" class → all "go into the washer"

---

- Function: an imperative sentence with the adverbial postposed
- Mathematical sense: the `f` in `y = f(x)`
- In R, a function is both a mathematical mapping and a set of actions
- Seen already: `c()`, `matrix()`, `mean()`

```{webr}
#| label: function

# Create a vector from 1 to 10
x <- 1:10

# Apply mean(), store the result as y
y <- mean(x)

y
```

---

- R function syntax:

`<result_name> <- <function>(<target_data>, <condition_1>, <condition_2>, ...)`

- Meaning: apply function on target data under conditions, then store the result
- Equivalent to "imperative with postposed adverbial" in English

---

- Custom function: `function(<inputs>){<actions>}`
- Example: a BMI calculator (BMI = weight / height²)

```{webr}
#| label: bmi

# Define a function that tells R how to compute BMI
# Inputs: weight, height
calculateBMI <- function(weight, height) {
  weight / (height^2)
}

# Compute BMI for someone weighing 80 kg and 1.69 m tall
calculateBMI(80, 1.69)
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Auto-printing"}

- Typing a function name directly triggers auto-print: the result appears in the console
- Pro: immediate visual feedback
- Con: the result is not saved, so it cannot be reused later

:::

---

- Function naming rules:
    - Must start with a letter
    - Only letters, digits, `.`, and `_` are allowed
    - Case-sensitive

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Why these naming rules"}

- Starts with a letter: lets the computer distinguish "a name" from "a number"
- No special characters: avoids collisions with reserved programming symbols
- Case-sensitive: required for precision

:::

---

- Naming conventions:
    - Verbs or verb phrases: `read_rds`, `saveRDS`
    - Nouns: `lm` (linear model), `dotwhisker`
    - Common abbreviations: `sqrt` (square root), `exp` (exponential)

---

- Package: a collection of functions
- Analogy: a toolbox with screwdrivers, hammers, wrenches
- Two sources:
    - CRAN (Comprehensive R Archive Network): reviewed and standardized
    - GitHub and other open platforms: no review, includes developer versions

---

- Load packages with `library()` or `require()`
- `require()` additionally checks whether the package is loaded; returns a logical value
- Install and load demo:

```r
# Install from CRAN (one-time only)
install.packages("drhur")

# Install from GitHub (developer version)
remotes::install_github("sammo3182/drhur")

# Load the package (do this every time R restarts)
library(drhur)

# Open the chapter tutorial inside drhur
drhur("play")
```

---

- Help docs can be accessed in several ways:
    - `?<function>`: e.g., `?install.packages`
    - `help(<function>)`: equivalent
    - `help.search("<keyword>")`: search across docs
    - `help.start()`: opens the top-level help (rarely used)

---

::: {.callout-tip title="How long does a package install take?"}

- Usually a few seconds to a dozen seconds
- The first install also fetches dependencies, which can take longer
- A screen full of scrolling messages is normal — just wait it out

:::

---

- Built on OOP, R has four key properties:
    - Inheritance
    - Encapsulation
    - Polymorphism
    - Safety

---

- Inheritance: a subclass inherits all properties of its parent
- Check with `inherits(<object>, "<class_name>")`
- Example: ordered factors created by `ordered()` inherit from `factor`

```{webr}
#| label: inherit

# A nominal factor
x <- factor(c("A", "B", "C", "D"))
# An ordinal factor
y <- ordered(c("A", "B", "C", "D"))

# Is x a factor?
inherits(x, "factor")
# Is y a factor? (TRUE, because ordered inherits from factor)
inherits(y, "factor")
```

---

- Encapsulation: bundle a series of commands into a single command
- `function()` is the most common encapsulation tool

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Encapsulation is overwritable"}

- In R, functions are also objects — "anything can be a function"
- Existing functions, including base R built-ins, can be overwritten
- Danger: `mean <- function(x){x^2}` would make all subsequent `mean()` calls compute squares
- Avoid overwriting / modifying / deleting built-in commands

:::

---

- Polymorphism: one function behaves differently on different object types
- Canonical example: `summary()` adapts to its input

```{webr}
#| label: polymorphism

# Categorical vector x and numeric vector y
x <- factor(c("A", "B", "C", "D"))
y <- 1:10

# summary() on a factor: frequency table
summary(x)

# summary() on numeric: min, max, mean, etc.
summary(y)
```

---

- Safety: R checks class compatibility before applying a command
- Incompatible classes stop execution and raise an error; subsequent commands do not run

```{webr}
#| label: safety
#| error: true

# Mismatched types: number + string
5 + "hello"

# This line will not be executed because of the error above
1 + 1
```

---

::: {.callout-important title="Command, method, and function"}

- *Command*: any executable line of code (`1 + 1`, `y <- sqrt(x)`)
- *Function*: a fixed set of operations that takes inputs and returns a result
- *Method*: a function specialized for a particular class

:::

---

- OOP principle: *do not touch the raw data directly*
- Process data through "object + command" patterns
- Advantages: every step is traceable, safer, and easier to share

## Section Exercises {.unnumbered}

**Exercise 1**: Install multiple packages at once

`install.packages` uses the plural noun "packages" — it can install several packages in one call. Use the help doc to find out how to install `regioncode`, `dotwhisker`, and `drhur` at the same time.

::: { .hint exercise="install_multi" }

- Read `?install.packages`, especially the `pkgs` argument
- That argument accepts a character vector
- Use `c()` to bundle multiple package names

:::

```{webr}
#| label: install_multi
#| exercise: install_multi

# Try installing the three packages at once
?install.packages

```

::: { .solution exercise="install_multi" }

```r
?install.packages

# Bundle the three names into a character vector with c(),
# then pass it to install.packages() in a single call.
# This vectorized form is far cleaner than three separate calls.
install.packages(c("regioncode", "dotwhisker", "drhur"))
```

:::

---

**Exercise 2**: Interpret the polymorphism of `plot`

Run the lines below and explain, from the perspective of polymorphism, why the same `plot()` function produces different plots.

::: { .hint exercise="plot_polymorphism" }

- Behavior depends on the number of arguments
- Single argument → uses the index as the x-axis
- Two arguments → builds a scatter plot

:::

```{webr}
#| label: plot_polymorphism
#| exercise: plot_polymorphism

plot(mtcars$mpg)

plot(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$wt)

```

::: { .solution exercise="plot_polymorphism" }

```r
# Single-argument call: index (1, 2, 3, ...) as x-axis, vector values as y-axis
plot(mtcars$mpg)

# Two-argument call: first vector is x, second vector is y → scatter plot
plot(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$wt)

# The same plot() function contains multiple internal "plans"
# It selects a plan based on the number and type of arguments
# That selection logic is polymorphism in action
```

:::

## Records (Data Storage)

- Creating data in R = converting real-world observations into analyzable form
- R organizes data by class, each class with its own properties and methods
- Common classes: vector (`vector`), data frame (`data.frame`), list (`list`)

---

- Assignment: give a value or object a "name" and cache it for reuse
- Standard operator: `<-`
- Equivalent forms: `assign()`, `->`, `<<-`, `=`
- Basic syntax: `<name> <- <object>`

```{webr}
#| label: object

# Store 1-5 into a variable named aValidObject
aValidObject <- 1:5

# Print the variable
aValidObject
```

---

- `<-` and `->` point in opposite directions; the arrow indicates the name

```{webr}
#| label: arrow

# Standard form: store 12 into a
a <- 12

# Reverse form: store 25 into b
25 -> b
```

---

- `=` also assigns, but with subtle differences from `<-`:
    - Top-level: both behave the same
    - Inside function calls: `=` is for naming arguments; `<-` is for creating/modifying objects

```{webr}
#| label: diffAssign
#| error: true

# With <-: y is created in the global environment; printing y works
median(y <- 1:10); y

# With =: x is local to median(); printing outside fails
median(x = 1:10); x
```

---

- `<<-` refers to or modifies a parent (global) variable
- Useful when distinguishing scopes in nested environments
- Example: campus bulletin (global) vs. personal reminder (local)

```{webr}
#| label: globalAssign

# Campus bulletin (global)
campus_bulletin <- "Tonight's lecture is at 7 PM in the main hall."

# Personal reminder: <- creates a local variable; global is untouched
write_personal_reminder <- function() {
  campus_bulletin <- "Finish homework before the lecture!"
  message("Personal reminder: ", campus_bulletin)
}

# Bulletin updater: <<- modifies the global variable
update_campus_bulletin <- function() {
  campus_bulletin <<- "LECTURE CANCELED: The professor is sick. Free time tonight!"
  message("The campus bulletin has a new announcement.")
}

# Check the bulletin
message("Bulletin: ", campus_bulletin)

# Write a personal reminder (does not affect the global bulletin)
write_personal_reminder()
message("Bulletin: ", campus_bulletin)

# Update the global bulletin
update_campus_bulletin()
message("Bulletin: ", campus_bulletin)
```

---

- Object naming rules:
    - Begin with a letter or `.` (if `.`, the next character cannot be a digit)
    - Cannot begin with a digit
    - Only letters, digits, `.`, `_`
    - Case-sensitive: `X != x`
    - Avoid overwriting built-in commands (e.g., `list`, `mean`)

---

- Vector: any "sequence" of data in R
- Built with `c()` or `vector()`
- Strictly homogeneous: all elements must be the same type
- Subtypes: numeric, character, logical, factor, date/time (POSIXct/POSIXlt)

---

- Numeric vector: values that allow algebraic operations
- Subtypes: double (decimals allowed), integer

```{webr}
#| label: numeric

# Double-precision vector
vec_double <- c(1.5, -2.34, 1/3)

# Integer-style vector
vec_integer <- c(1, -2, 4, 6)

# Sequence
vec_1_100 <- c(1:100)

# Vector with missing values (NA)
vec_miss <- c(1, NA, 3, NA, 24)
```

---

::: {.callout-important title="Missing value NA"}

- `NA` stands for "not available" — R's representation of missing data
- A missing value may come from human omission or from a computation that did not produce a result
- It is the legal exception to R's homogeneity rule for vectors

:::

---

- Logical vector: binary `TRUE` / `FALSE` (or `T` / `F`)
- Used everywhere — much of programming is really "test a condition, branch"
- `as.logical()` converts to logical

```{webr}
#| label: logic

# Numeric vector (0 = FALSE, non-0 = TRUE)
vec_num <- c(1, 2, 0, 1, 1)

# Coerce to logical
vec_logic <- as.logical(vec_num)

vec_logic
```

---

- Factor vector: stores categorical information
- Created with `factor()`; the `levels` attribute records the categories, `labels` names them
- Subtype: ordered factor (ordinal), created with `ordered()`, includes order information

```{webr}
#| label: factor

# Base factor: levels auto-detected (1, 2, 3)
vec_fac <- factor(c(1, 2, 2, 3))

# Advanced factor: custom levels and labels
vec_fac2 <- factor(
  c(1, 2, 2, 3),
  levels = c(3, 2, 1),
  labels = c("Apple", "Pear", "Orange")
)

# Ordered factor: levels imply order
vec_ord <- ordered(c(1, 2, 2, 3))

vec_fac
vec_fac2
vec_ord
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="The `forcats` package"}

- Specialized for working with categorical and ordinal variables
- Concise functions for reordering levels, adding categories, changing reference groups
- Will be revisited in later chapters with concrete examples

:::

---

- Character vector: stores text values
- R does not "read" the content of characters — treats Chinese, English, and Russian alike
- Wrap each element in quotes when creating

```{webr}
#| label: character

# Character vector (note the quotes)
vec_chr <- c("牛", "♥️", "R is hard, but I can nail it.")

vec_chr
```

---

- Pre-stored character constants: `letters`, `LETTERS`, `month.abb`, `month.name`

---

- Date/time vectors: R offers `POSIXct` and `POSIXlt`
- `POSIXct`: stored as seconds since 1970-01-01
- `POSIXlt`: stored as a list of time components
- Current system time: `Sys.time()`, `Sys.Date()`, `Sys.timezone()`

```{webr}
#| label: time

# POSIXct: seconds-based storage
vector_ct <- as.POSIXct("2023-03-20 10:11:12")

# POSIXlt: list-based storage
vector_lt <- as.POSIXlt("2023-03-20 10:11:12")

# Both print identically though the underlying storage differs
vector_ct
vector_lt

# System time and date
Sys.time()
Sys.Date()
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="The `lubridate` package"}

- Specialized for working with date/time data
- Handles diverse formats: different field orders, time zone conversions

:::

```{webr}
#| label: lubridate

library(lubridate)

# "Year-Month-Day"
ymd("20221016")
# "Month-Day-Year"
mdy("10-16-2022")
# "Day-Month-Year"
dmy("16/10/2022")
# "Year-Month-Day Hour:Minute:Second" with timezone
ymd_hms("2022-10-16 09:00:00", tz = "Etc/GMT+8")
```

---

- Data frame: two-dimensional structure with equal-length columns; columns may differ in type
- In practice, "the dataset" usually refers to a data frame
- Two extraction methods:
    - Bracket form: `df[row, col]`
    - Dollar-sign form: `df$column` (the most common)

```{webr}
#| label: dataframe

# Create a data frame with logical, numeric, and character columns
df_toy <- data.frame(
  female = c(FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),
  age = c(29, 39, 38, 12),
  name = c("Iron Man", "Black Widow", "Captain Marvel", "Captain America")
)

# Inspect
df_toy

# Bracket form: row 3, column 3
df_toy[3, 3]

# Dollar-sign form: the entire 'name' column
df_toy$name
```

---

- List: a heterogeneous container; elements can vary in size, length, order
- A data frame is a special list: columns are equal-length and aligned row by row

```{webr}
#| label: list

# A list with three components of different lengths
ls_monks <- list(
  name = c("Wukong Sun", "Sanzang Tang", "Wuneng Zhu", "Wujing Sha"),
  power = c(100, 20, 90, 40, 26, 78, 10000),
  buddha = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE)
)

# Inspect
ls_monks

# Extract the 'name' component
ls_monks$name
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Arrays for multi-dimensional data"}

- Built with `array()`, each dimension storing one vector
- Rarely needed in social-science applications

:::

```{webr}
#| label: array

# A 3x3x2 three-dimensional array
an_array <- array(c(5, 9, 3, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15), dim = c(3, 3, 2))

an_array
```

---

- Probe-style tools for inspecting attributes:
    - `class()` / `typeof()`: variable class / underlying storage
    - `nchar()`: character string length
    - `levels()`: factor levels
    - `length()`: length (elements for vectors, components for lists, columns for data frames)
    - `nrow()` / `ncol()` / `dim()`: dimensions
    - `summary()`: summary statistics

```{webr}
#| label: probe

# Class and underlying type
class(vec_double)
typeof(vec_integer)

# String length and factor levels
nchar(vec_chr)
levels(vec_fac2)

# length() means different things for different objects
length(vec_double)
length(ls_monks)
length(df_toy)

# Data frame dimensions
nrow(df_toy)
ncol(df_toy)
dim(df_toy)
```

---

- `class()`: what R "sees" the object as → which functions can apply
- `typeof()`: how the object is stored at the byte level
- `length()` on a data frame returns the number of columns (since a df is a list)
- For row count use `nrow()`, for columns `ncol()`, for both `dim()`

---

- Predicate-style tools: usually prefixed with `is`
- Examples: `is.numeric()`, `is.character()`, `is.factor()`, `is.data.frame()`, `is.list()`
- Return a logical value indicating whether the object belongs to the given class

## Section Exercises {.unnumbered}

**Exercise 3**: Naming rules

The two lines below: the first errors, the second runs. Explain why.

::: { .hint exercise="naming" }

- One naming rule: object names cannot begin with a digit
- Which rule does the first name violate?
- Is the second name compliant?

:::

```{webr}
#| label: naming
#| exercise: naming

5var_name <- 1:5

var_name5 <- 1:5

```

::: { .solution exercise="naming" }

```r
# Line 1: 5var_name <- 1:5
# Error reason: R forbids names that begin with a digit
# "5var_name" starts with 5 — rule violation

# Line 2: var_name5 <- 1:5
# Valid: starts with letter v, contains only letters, digits, underscore
var_name5
```

:::

---

**Exercise 4**: Two ways to build a letter sequence

Compare the two vectors below and verify whether they are identical.

::: { .hint exercise="letters_compare" }

- `c()` requires typing each character by hand
- `letters` is R's pre-stored lowercase Latin alphabet
- Use `identical()` to compare two vectors

:::

```{webr}
#| label: letters_compare
#| exercise: letters_compare

vec_letters_raw <- c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e")

vec_letters_quick <- letters[1:5]

```

::: { .solution exercise="letters_compare" }

```r
vec_letters_raw <- c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e")
vec_letters_quick <- letters[1:5]

# Verify the two are identical
identical(vec_letters_raw, vec_letters_quick)

# Results are identical: both five-character vectors "a"-"e"
# vec_letters_raw: manual entry, useful for ad hoc characters
# vec_letters_quick: uses pre-stored constant, more concise for alphabetical sequences
```

:::

---

**Exercise 5**: Parse messy date strings

`lubridate::parse_date_time()` can parse a vector containing mixed date formats. Use it to convert the following character vector into standard date-time values:

`c("20190101", "01012019", "021901")`

::: { .hint exercise="date_parse" }

- Load `lubridate`
- The `orders` argument takes a vector of format guesses
- Try `c("ymd", "dmy", "mdy")` as the guess list

:::

```{webr}
#| label: date_parse
#| exercise: date_parse

library(lubridate)
raw_dates <- c("20190101", "01012019", "021901")

```

::: { .solution exercise="date_parse" }

```r
library(lubridate)
raw_dates <- c("20190101", "01012019", "021901")

# orders supplies a list of format guesses
# The function tries each and produces standard POSIXct values
parsed_dates <- parse_date_time(raw_dates, orders = c("ymd", "dmy", "mdy"))
parsed_dates
```

:::

---

**Exercise 6**: Explore the wvs7 dataset

- World Values Survey (WVS): a cross-national survey launched in 1981
- WVS7 is the seventh wave
- The `drhur` package bundles a 2% sample (1264 observations, 24 variables) as `wvs7`

Use what you have learned to inspect its basic structure:

a. What is the class of `wvs7` itself?
b. How many rows and columns?
c. Extract the `marital` column — what levels does it contain?
d. Use `is.*` predicates to verify: is `wvs7` a data frame? a list? a matrix?

::: { .hint exercise="wvs7_explore" }

- `class()` for type
- `dim()`, `nrow()`, `ncol()` for dimensions
- `$` to extract a column
- `levels()` for factor levels
- `is.data.frame()`, `is.list()`, `is.matrix()` for verification

:::

```{webr}
#| label: wvs7_explore
#| exercise: wvs7_explore

# Load the wvs7 dataset
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

```

::: { .solution exercise="wvs7_explore" }

```r
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# a. class
class(wvs7)
# Result includes "tbl_df" "tbl" "data.frame"

# b. dimensions
dim(wvs7)        # 1264   24
nrow(wvs7)
ncol(wvs7)

# c. marital column
head(wvs7$marital)
levels(wvs7$marital)

# d. structure verification
is.data.frame(wvs7)  # TRUE
is.list(wvs7)        # TRUE (a data frame is a list under the hood)
is.matrix(wvs7)      # FALSE (unless explicitly coerced)
```

:::

## Input {#sec-sub_input-en}

- In practice we rarely hand-create data; we usually import from external sources
- Three input paths: built-in datasets, direct reading, package-based reading

---

- Built-in data: bundled with R or with R packages
- List all: `data()`
- Load one: `data(<name>)`
- Load from a package: `data(<name>, package = "<package_name>")`

```{webr}
#| label: loadData_builtin

# Load the built-in mtcars dataset
data(mtcars)

# Inspect summary
summary(mtcars)
```

---

- For a dataset inside another package, pass the `package` argument

```r
# Without loading regioncode, this fails
data(corruption)

# Explicitly point R to the regioncode package
data(corruption, package = "regioncode")

summary(corruption)
```

---

- R's built-in `datasets` package: no install or load needed
- Examples: `iris`, `mtcars`, `uspop`

```{webr}
#| label: load_iris

# datasets is loaded by default
data(iris)

# Inspect first six rows
head(iris)
```

---

- Direct reading supports these formats:
    - `.RDS`: R-native, single object
    - `.RData`: R-native, multiple objects
    - `.txt`: delimited tables
    - `.csv`: comma-separated values
- Input syntax: `<name> <- <read_function>(<path>)`
- Read functions typically start with `read`

```r
# RDS
df_rds <- readRDS("aDataset.rds")

# TXT (general table)
df_txt <- read.table("D:/aDataset.txt")

# CSV
df_csv <- read.csv("./aDataset.csv")

# RData: no need to assign to an object
load("aDataset.RData")
```

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Filename case sensitivity"}

- Windows / macOS: `.RData` and `.rdata` are treated the same
- Linux: strictly case-sensitive — they are two different files
- Recommendation: use lowercase `.rds` and `.rdata` consistently

:::

---

- Specialized packages for other formats:
    - `haven`: SPSS / Stata / SAS
    - `readr`: CSV / TXT (faster than base R)
    - `readxl`: Excel
    - `rjson`: JSON
    - `XML`: XML / HTML

```r
# --- Statistical software files ---
library(haven)
df_spss <- read_spss("<FileName>.sav")
df_stata <- read_dta("<FileName>.dta")
df_sas <- read_sas("<FileName>.sas7bdat")

# --- Plain-text tables ---
library(readr)
df_csv <- read_csv("<FileName>.csv")
df_table <- read_table("<FileName>.txt")

# --- Excel ---
library(readxl)
df_excel <- read_excel("<FileName>.xlsx")

# --- JSON ---
library(rjson)
df_json <- fromJSON(file = "<FileName>.json")

# --- XML / HTML ---
library(XML)
df_xml <- xmlTreeParse("<url>")
df_html <- readHTMLTable("<url>", which = 3)
```

---

- The Swiss-army knife: `rio::import()`
- Auto-detects format — one function for many file types
- Trade-off: slightly slower than format-specific calls

```r
library(rio)
df_anything <- import("<AnyTypeOfData>")
```

## Section Exercises {.unnumbered}

**Exercise 7**: Load the built-in `uspop`

Load the built-in dataset `uspop` and display its summary statistics.

::: { .hint exercise="uspop" }

- Use `data()` to load
- Use `summary()` to inspect

:::

```{webr}
#| label: uspop
#| exercise: uspop

```

::: { .solution exercise="uspop" }

```r
data(uspop)
summary(uspop)

# uspop is the US population time series from 1790 to 1970
# summary() recognizes a numeric vector and outputs:
# - Range: from 3.93 (1790) to 203.20 (1970)
# - Central tendency: mean 69.77, median 50.16
# - Mean > median → right-skewed (consistent with exponential growth)
# Another instance of polymorphism: summary() adapts to numeric input
```

:::

## Output

- Basic syntax: `<function>(<object>, <path>, <other_options>)`
- Function names typically start with `save` or `write`

---

- R-native formats:
    - `.rds`: single object, written with `saveRDS()`
    - `.RData`: multiple objects, written with `save()`

```r
# Save one object as RDS
saveRDS(df_toy, file = "df_toy.rds")

# Save multiple objects in a single RData file
save(df_toy, ls_monks, file = "./data/test.rdata")
```

- Recommendation: prefer `.rds` — clearer to load and manage
- `.RData` contents are only visible after `load()`

---

- CSV: cross-platform, universal
- Less compact than other formats, but every program reads it
- Write with `write.csv()`

```r
# Save df_toy as CSV
write.csv(df_toy, file = "toy.csv")
```

---

::: {.callout-warning title="CSV and non-ASCII data"}

- When the data contain Chinese (or other non-ASCII) characters, choose UTF-8 encoding
- Otherwise the file may display as garbled text

:::

---

- Other formats: use `rio::export()`
- Auto-detects file extension; exports to STATA, SPSS, SAS, Excel, JSON, Matlab, HTML, etc.

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Storage efficiency"}

- For small to mid-sized datasets, `.RData` is usually sufficient
- For very large datasets, consider specialized formats like `qs` or `fst`
- Specialized formats have weaker compatibility and storage stability (a file might fail to open on a different machine)

:::

## Chapter Recap

::: {.callout-note title="A friendly reminder"}

This chapter and the chapters that follow will cover quite a few concepts.
Some of them you do not need to master at first sight — a general impression is enough, and many will deepen as you encounter them again in later chapters.
Others, however, form the core foundation of this stage. Missing them now will hinder your understanding later.
Each chapter therefore ends with a key-point recap that flags what you must hold onto at this moment.

:::

---

- Foundational concepts
    - OOP (classes and objects)
    - Functions and assignment
    - Packages
- Data types
    - Vectors (numeric, logical, factor, character, date/time)
    - Matrices
    - Data frames and lists (key content)
- Data attributes: type, dimensions, summary
- Data input
    - Built-in datasets
    - Direct reading
    - Package-based reading (`haven`, `rio`, etc.)
- Data output
    - Save as R-native formats
    - Save as other formats

---

- Key functions
    - Basic operations and package management: `install.packages()`, `library()`, `help()` / `?`
    - Data creation and coercion: `c()`, `data.frame()`, `list()`, `as.numeric()`, `as.character()`, `as.logical()`
    - Data attributes: `class()`, `summary()`, `dim()`, `nrow()`, `ncol()`, `length()`, `head()`
    - Data I/O: `data()`, `read.csv()`, `readRDS()`, `saveRDS()`, `write.csv()`
